title: Fry bake
subtitle: Trinidadian fried breakfast bread
excerpt: Soft, pillowy fried bread rounds made with a simple baking powder dough. The Trinidadian breakfast staple eaten with buljol (salt cod salad), fried egg, or avocado.
sourceNotes: Fry bake is the standard Trinidadian breakfast fried bread, distinct from the Jamaican johnny cake in that it uses baking powder rather than yeast, giving a softer, more immediate result. It is eaten with buljol (a salt cod, tomato, and pepper salad), fried egg, saltfish, or avocado. The dough is very simple; the skill is in controlling the oil temperature so the outside puffs and colours without the inside remaining raw.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be shaped and refrigerated for up to 1 hour before frying.

content.text: Fry bake is made every morning in Trinidadian households. The dough takes 10 minutes to mix, needs 15 minutes to rest, and then fries in under 5 minutes per batch. The result is a soft, pillowy disc of fried bread that puffs in the hot oil and deflates slightly when you lift it out — inside it is almost hollow, which means it can be split and filled.

The oil temperature is the critical variable. Too hot and the outside browns before the inside cooks; too cool and the dough absorbs oil without puffing. A piece of raw dough dropped into the oil should sizzle immediately and float to the surface. The right heat gives a pale golden colour in 2 minutes per side — not deep brown.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Add the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil and rub in with your fingers. Add 200–220 ml of warm water, a little at a time, mixing until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms. Knead gently for 1 minute — do not overwork it. Cover and rest for 15 minutes.

Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces. With lightly oiled hands, flatten each piece into a round about 10 cm in diameter and 1 cm thick. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a saucepan to 170°C — test with a small piece of dough, which should sizzle and float immediately. Fry 2–3 fry bakes at a time for 2 minutes per side until pale golden and puffed. Lift out with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Oil temperature too low; dough too thin
items.fix: The oil must be at 170°C before the first batch goes in. Each fry bake should be at least 1 cm thick before frying.

items.symptom: Raw doughy centre despite golden outside
items.cause: Oil too hot; rounds too thick
items.fix: Lower the heat and fry for a longer time at a lower temperature. Flatten the rounds to 1 cm — not thicker.

items.cause: Dough overworked; not enough baking powder
items.fix: Knead for 1 minute only. The gluten should be just developed enough to hold the dough together.

items.cause: Oil temperature too low; not drained long enough
items.fix: Drain on a thick layer of kitchen paper for at least 60 seconds. A 170°C oil temperature prevents oil absorption.

content.text: Fry bake is the Trinidadian breakfast, eaten with buljol (a salad of flaked salt cod, tomato, spring onion, and pepper dressed with lime), a fried egg, avocado, or any combination of the three. The hollow centre, created by the puffing dough, makes it easy to fill or to tear open and use as a scoop. On the weekend it extends into brunch territory; at Carnival it becomes an all-hours food. It is one of the simplest things to cook and, eaten fresh from the oil with a cup of cocoa tea, very good.
